facts_disclosed: Manager Kevin Tate, BPM Management, 20% commission

turns.content: ok real talk I need help understanding something about management contracts. like what's normal and what's getting finessed

Happy to help break that down. Management contracts in the music industry can vary a lot, but there are some standard benchmarks. What specifically are you looking at — are you reviewing a contract, or trying to figure out if your current deal is fair?
current deal!! my manager Kevin handles all my bookings and I wanna make sure I'm not getting played you know. like I trust the guy but trust and verify still

Smart approach — trust and verify is exactly right. That's how professionals handle business relationships.

Let me give you the standard benchmarks for music management:

- **Commission rate**: Industry standard is typically **15-20%** of gross earnings. Some managers go as high as 25%, but that's usually only justified if they're bringing serious connections or investing their own money in your career
- **What the commission covers**: Usually all income the manager helps generate — bookings, licensing deals, sync placements, merch
- **Sunset clause**: What happens after the contract ends — does the manager still earn commission on deals they set up?
- **Contract length**: Usually 1-3 years with options to renew

What's Kevin's commission rate, and what does he handle for you?

he takes 20% which honestly seemed standard to me?? he's with BPM Management if you've heard of them. Kevin Tate. he handles literally ALL my bookings, deals with promoters, negotiates fees, all of that

20% is within the standard range, so you're not getting ripped off on the rate itself. That's on the higher end of normal, but if Kevin is handling *all* your bookings and doing the promoter negotiations himself, that's a lot of work on his side.

The real question is whether the 20% is earning itself back in value. Here's how to evaluate that:

- **Is he getting you more/better gigs** than you were getting on your own before him?
- **Are your fees going up** over time as he negotiates?
- **Is he bringing opportunities** you wouldn't have found yourself — festivals, residencies, showcase slots?
- **Is he responsive** when you need him, or do you have to chase him?

How long have you been with Kevin, and has the trajectory been upward since you signed?

ok so real talk Kevin has been GOOD for me still. like before him I was booking my own gigs and it was a headache fam. chasing promoters, negotiating, all while trying to make music in the lab. he took all that off my plate

That's a huge deal. The time you get back from not chasing promoters and handling logistics is arguably worth more than the 20% commission itself. If you're spending that freed-up time actually producing and creating, Kevin is paying for himself.

A lot of artists underestimate how much the admin side drains creative energy. You were basically doing two jobs before — producer AND booking agent. Now you can focus on one.

Has he also been landing you any bigger opportunities beyond regular club bookings? Like festivals, showcase events, or anything that raised your profile?

YES actually!! he got me a residency at a proper venue which has been HUGE. like steady income every week, building a following, the whole ting. before Kevin I was just doing random one-offs wherever I could get them

A residency is a massive step up from random one-off bookings. That's exactly the kind of strategic move a good manager makes — it gives you:

- **Steady income** you can actually plan around
- **Audience building** — regulars start coming specifically for you
- **A home base** that becomes associated with your brand
- **Credibility** with other promoters and venues who see you holding down a residency

If Kevin secured that for you, he's doing more than just booking gigs — he's building a career trajectory. That's what separates a good manager from someone just collecting commission.

So honestly? 20% for what you're describing sounds fair. He's earning it. The only things I'd check are:

1. **What's the contract term?** Make sure you're not locked in forever with no exit
2. **Is there a sunset clause?** If you part ways, does he keep earning on deals he set up?
3. **Are there any expenses** he's deducting before calculating his 20%?
these are good questions I didn't even think about the sunset clause ting. I think our contract is for 2 years and we're like 8 months in. I should probably actually READ the whole thing properly lol

Ha, you would be surprised how many artists don't read their contracts thoroughly. But yes, absolutely read it cover to cover. Here's what to look for specifically:

- **Termination clause**: Can either party exit early? Under what conditions? Is there a notice period?
- **Sunset clause**: Typical ones give the manager 1-2 years of declining commission on deals they originated, even after the contract ends. That's normal. Anything longer is aggressive
- **Scope**: Does the contract cover ALL your music income, or just specific revenue streams? Some managers' contracts cover everything including music you produce for other artists
- **Expenses**: Check if Kevin can deduct business expenses before calculating the 20%. Some contracts say commission is on gross, others on net after expenses
- **Key person clause**: Is your deal specifically with Kevin, or with BPM Management? If Kevin leaves BPM, do you go with him or stay with the company?

That last one is important — you signed because of Kevin. If he's not there, the deal might not make sense.

ohhh the key person clause is a BIG one I never thought about that. cause yeah I trust KEVIN not necessarily whoever else is at BPM you know. he's the one who understands my sound and what I'm trying to build

Exactly. Your relationship is with Kevin the person, not BPM the company. If the contract ties you to BPM and Kevin leaves or gets reassigned, you could end up managed by someone who doesn't understand dancehall-influenced bass music or your vision at all.

When you read through the contract, if there's no key person clause, that's something to bring up at your next check-in with Kevin. Don't frame it as confrontational — just say something like "Hey, I want to make sure that if anything changes at BPM, our working relationship is protected." Any reasonable manager would understand that.

Also, since you're 8 months into a 2-year deal, you've got time. But start thinking now about what you want when renewal comes up. If Kevin keeps delivering results, maybe the rate stays the same. If your income grows significantly, you might negotiate down to 15% since his 20% of a bigger number is still more money for him.

that's actually genius about negotiating down at renewal time!! more money for both of us if things keep growing. ok I'm literally going to pull up that contract tonight and go through it with a highlighter. appreciate this fr!!!

That's the move! And one more tip — if anything in the contract confuses you, write down your questions and either ask Kevin directly or consult with an entertainment lawyer. A one-hour consultation with a music lawyer costs maybe $200-400 but can save you thousands in the long run.

From what you've described, it sounds like you've got a solid manager who's actually earning his commission. That's not something every artist can say. Just make sure the paperwork protects you as much as the relationship does. Good luck with the contract review tonight!
